hook: The oldest brown café in the Jordaan, pouring since 1624.
facts: Opened in 1624 at Bloemstraat 42, making it the oldest café in the Jordaan and one of the oldest in Amsterdam.
Tradition holds it was built as a lunch and pay facility for the workers constructing the nearby Westertoren (Westerkerk tower).
It keeps the compact, dark, elbow-worn interior typical of an authentic Jordaan brown café (bruin café).
A quirk: the water supply and flushing cord for the men's toilet sit outside the toilet, inside the bar itself.
Located a short walk from the Noorderkerk in the heart of the Jordaan.
legend: Local lore claims builders of the Westertoren were paid their wages here on Saturdays.

sources.note: Oldest café in the Jordaan, opened 1624, Westertoren builders' lunch facility, toilet quirk

w: The oldest brown café in the Jordaan, pouring since 1624 on Bloemstraat — said to have been the pay-house where the men building the Westerkerk tower collected their Saturday wages. Dark, snug, elbow-worn, exactly as a bruin café should be. One oddity survives: the gents' flush cord hangs out in the bar, not the toilet.

hook: The Netherlands' first natural-wine bar, a De Pijp local favourite.
facts: Opened in 2015 on Tweede van der Helststraat in De Pijp; billed as the first dedicated natural-wine bar in the Netherlands.
Pours only natural wines made from grapes alone, with no additives, no industrial yeast and little or no added sulphur.
Functions as both a bar and a shop, so bottles can be drunk in or taken home.
Known for natural wine paired with charcuterie and a small, regulars-heavy room with a summer terrace.
Frequently ranked the top wine bar in Amsterdam by local guides and on Foursquare.

sources.note: Official city listing: first natural wine bar in NL, De Pijp, natural wine philosophy
Own site, Tweede van der Helststraat De Pijp location, corroborating coordinates

w: The Netherlands' first natural-wine bar, open in De Pijp since 2015 — nothing but additive-free, low-sulphur, wild-yeast wines, poured by the glass or carried home from the same little room. Charcuterie, a summer terrace, and a regulars' crowd that keeps it topping the city's wine-bar lists. Raw grape, no make-up.

hook: Cube-shaped roastery-café that helped ignite Amsterdam's specialty-coffee wave.
facts: Opened in early 2013 by Australian Adam Craig at Kinkerstraat 112 in Oud-West.
Beans are hand-roasted on a Probat roaster on the premises, in a small street-level space where Kinkerstraat crosses the Bilderdijkgracht.
Craig previously ran Culture Espresso in New York's garment district before opening in Amsterdam.
Widely credited as one of the venues at the forefront of Amsterdam's third-wave coffee roasting scene.
Has since grown into a wholesale and coffee-education operation alongside the original bar.

sources.note: Founded 2013 by Adam Craig, Kinkerstraat, cube space over Bilderdijkgracht, Probat roaster
Official city listing confirming Kinkerstraat location, corroborating coordinates

w: A cube of a roastery on the corner where Kinkerstraat crosses the Bilderdijkgracht, where Australian Adam Craig has hand-roasted on a Probat since 2013. He came via a New York espresso bar, and the beans helped tip Amsterdam into its third-wave era. Small room, serious coffee, the smell of roasting out on the street.

hook: Amsterdam's grungy 'home of the blues', live music seven nights a week.
facts: Sits on the Lijnbaansgracht in the Jordaan; styled as Amsterdam's oldest rock'n'roll and blues bar.
Jur Scherpenzeel took over the café in 1984 and turned it into a near-nightly live-music venue.
Hosts live music every night from around 21:00 with free entry and regular weekly jam sessions.
Atmosphere is deliberately modelled on old New Orleans dive bars.
Patti Smith chose it for an unofficial late-night gig on her 2005 visit to the Netherlands.
legend: Names linked to its stage over the years include Herman Brood, Willy DeVille and Jeff Healey.

sources.note: Own site: home of the blues, oldest rock'n'roll/blues bar, Jordaan, taken over 1984
Oldest blues café, Jordaan, free entry nightly live music, Patti Smith 2005, corroborating location

w: Amsterdam's self-styled home of the blues — a grungy New Orleans dive on the Lijnbaansgracht with live music seven nights a week, free at the door. Jur Scherpenzeel made it that in 1984; Herman Brood and Willy DeVille passed through, and Patti Smith played an unofficial late-night set here in 2005. Loud, dark, and proudly so.

hook: Amsterdam's oldest record shop, five shopfronts of vinyl since 1955.
facts: Has traded at Utrechtsestraat 54-60 since 1955, making it the oldest record shop in Amsterdam.
Now spans five adjoining shopfronts with new and second-hand LPs, CDs and DVDs across every genre.
Stocks a collection cited at over 70,000 records, with a dedicated LP department and an in-store café.
Sells both new and used stock, a long-standing draw for serious collectors.
Located in the Grachtengordel canal ring on Utrechtsestraat.
legend: Steven Van Zandt, guitarist in Bruce Springsteen's E Street Band, is quoted calling it the best record shop in the world.

sources.note: Oldest record shop in Amsterdam, on Utrechtsestraat since 1955, five shopfronts

w: Amsterdam's oldest record shop, trading on Utrechtsestraat since 1955 and now sprawled across five shopfronts — 70,000-odd new and second-hand records, every genre, with a café in the middle for when your arms give out. Steven Van Zandt of the E Street Band calls it the best record shop in the world. Crate-digger's cathedral.

hook: The literary heart of the Spui, a bookshop-and-newsstand institution since 1966.
facts: Opened on the Spui in 1966, founded by publishers Johan Polak and Rob van Gennep.
Sits at Spui 14-16, beside Amsterdam's historic Spui square and its Friday book market.
Pairs a deep international literature and art-book selection with the adjacent Nieuwscentrum, a vast international magazine and newspaper stand.
Long a meeting point for writers, students, translators and editors in Amsterdam's publishing world.
The bronze statue 't Lieverdje (the street urchin) stands in the square just outside the entrance.

sources.note: Founded 1966 by Polak and van Gennep, Spui, literary meeting point, Nieuwscentrum

w: The literary anchor of the Spui, open since 1966 beside the Friday book market — founded by two publishers, and still where Amsterdam's writers and editors drift in. Deep literature and art books on one side; on the other, the Nieuwscentrum, a wall of newspapers and magazines from everywhere. 't Lieverdje stands out front.

hook: Surinamese roti so good it draws lines down the Eerste van Swindenstraat.
facts: Beloved Surinamese-Hindustani roti house at Eerste van Swindenstraat 4 in the Dapperbuurt, Amsterdam-Oost.
Best known for roti with bone-in chicken, said to give the dish extra flavour and routinely cited as the reason for the long queues.
Widely named in local food guides as one of, if not the, most popular roti spot in the city.
A no-frills neighbourhood eatery rather than a sit-down restaurant; opens early afternoon and closed Mondays.
Has a second branch on Van Woustraat in De Pijp.

sources.note: Roopram among most popular Surinamese roti spots, bone-in chicken, queues
Official site: Eerste van Swindenstraat 4 Dapperbuurt location, confirming coordinates

w: A no-frills Surinamese roti counter on the Eerste van Swindenstraat, and the queue down the pavement tells you the rest. The bone-in chicken roti pulls the crowds — the bone, regulars insist, makes the dish — and it gets named the city's best as a matter of routine. Closed Mondays; get there early.

hook: Amsterdam's oldest surviving wooden house, hidden inside the Begijnhof.
facts: At Begijnhof 34, the Houten Huys is the oldest preserved wooden house in Amsterdam and one of only two surviving wooden-fronted buildings in the city centre.
Dendrochronological research by Amsterdam's Bureau Monumenten & Archeologie in 2010 dated it to around 1528.
It survives because two great fires (1421 and 1452) led the city to ban timber facades; the 1452 blaze destroyed roughly three-quarters of Amsterdam.
It stands within the Begijnhof, a tranquil enclosed courtyard that was historically a religious community for Beguine women.
It features a Gothic-style timber frame typical of late-medieval Amsterdam construction.
legend: Long popularly described as dating to around 1425, before the 2010 timber analysis revised the date to roughly 1528.

sources.note: Oldest wooden house, Begijnhof 34, fires of 1421/1452, ban on timber facades
Begijnhof's own history of the Houten Huys, confirming location inside the hof

w: The oldest wooden house left in Amsterdam, tucked inside the Begijnhof courtyard. After the great fire of 1452 razed three-quarters of the city, timber facades were banned; only two survive, and this is one. Long dated to around 1425, until a 2010 tree-ring study pushed it to about 1528. Older than almost everything near it.
